data.title: Money Supply Grows For A 25th Consecutive Month
data.description:
Money Supply is a very important indicator. It helps show how tight or loose current monetary conditions are regardless of what the Fed is doing with interest rates. Even if the Fed is tight, if Money Supply is increasing, it has an inflationary effect.

One key metric shown below is the “Wenzel” 13-week annualized money supply figure. It was made popular by the late Robert Wenzel, who tracked the metric weekly as an indicator for where the economy might be headed. In 2020, the Fed started reporting the data monthly instead of weekly. It should also be noted that Money Supply data can be heavily revised in future months.

Recent Trends

Seasonally Adjusted Money Supply has been growing on a consistent monthly basis since November 2023 (25 straight months). The latest month (November) showed a modest increase of $24B. While this is the smallest growth since December of last year, it is still positive.

Figure 1: MoM M2 Change (Seasonally Adjusted)

The increase was 1.3% annualized, which is well below the 4.3% over the past year.

Figure 2: M2 Growth Rates

November average is typically around 4.5%, which puts the latest month well below average.

Figure 3: Average Monthly Growth Rates

Non-seasonally adjusted shows positive growth for the months of November and December (note: this data is ahead of the seasonally adjusted data). The last two months have been the strongest two months since June.

Figure 4: MoM M2 Change (Non-Seasonally Adjusted)

The weekly data shows what happened. There was a decent pop in the most recent week of $122B for the week of Dec 1st. Keep in mind, this is unadjusted, which is why it does not match Figure 1.

Figure 5: WoW M2 Change

The “Wenzel” 13-week Money Supply

The late Robert Wenzel of Economic Policy Journal used a modified calculation to track Money Supply. He used a trailing 13-week average growth rate annualized as defined in his book The Fed Flunks. He specifically used the weekly data that was not seasonally adjusted. His analogy was that in order to know what to wear outside, he wants to know the current weather, not temperatures that have been averaged throughout the year.

The objective of the 13-week average is to smooth some of the choppy data without bringing in too much history that could blind someone from seeing what’s in front of them. The 13-week average growth rate can be seen in the table below.

Growth was accelerating for 13 straight weeks but has slowed a touch to 5.08%. After a brief 3-week deceleration, there has been growth the last 2 weeks, which has put it back above 5%.

Figure 6: WoW Trailing 13-week Average Money Supply Growth

The plot below shows how this year compares with previous years. This year started at about the average point for the last 10 years and has stayed in that range the entire year. Growth is typically flat to up for the last two months of the year. It appears this year should fit in the same line. It wouldn’t be surprising to see a pop in the last few weeks of the year, which will be reported next month.

Figure 7: Yearly 13-week Overlay

Inflation and Money Supply

The chart below shows the history of inflation, Money Supply, and Fed Funds. As shown, in 1970 inflation worked with a ~2-year lag compared to Money Supply. Money Supply slowed dramatically in 2023 and 2024 but has been moving back up. Inflation has also been stickier than the Fed would like, but unfortunately, they cannot do much given the large debt load of the US Government and Corporations. Despite inflation staying elevated, even moving up in the recent period, the Fed has no choice but to continue lowering rates.

The inflation rate is now only 1.2% below the fed funds rate. That is not a big enough gap to slow inflation, especially when the money supply is still expanding.

Figure 8: YoY M2 Change with CPI and Fed Funds

Historical Perspective

The charts below are designed to put the current trends into historical perspective. The orange bars represent annualized percentage change rather than raw dollar amount.

Figure 9: M2 with Growth Rate

Below shows the 13-week annualized average over history. This chart overlays the log return of the S&P. Mr. Wenzel proposed that large drops in Money Supply could be a sign of stock market pullbacks. His theory, derived from Murray Rothbard, states that when the market experiences a shrinking growth rate of Money Supply (or even negative), it can create liquidity issues in the stock market, leading to a sell-off.

While not a perfect predictive tool, many of the dips in Money Supply precede market dips. Specifically, the major dips in 2002 and 2008 from +10% down to 0%. 2022 was highly correlated with a fall in Money Supply and the rebound has corresponded with the big stock market move we saw in 2023 and into 2024. The slowing growth in 2025 did correlate with the market drop in March/April, but that was clearly more headline driven. With money growth continuing to climb, it seems to be providing enough fuel to keep pushing the market higher. The current November market correction looks like it will likely be shallow since the money supply remains elevated

Please note the chart only shows market data through Dec 1st to align with available M2 data.

Figure 10: 13-week M2 Annualized and S&P 500

One other consideration is the reverse repo market at the Fed. This is a tool that allows financial institutions to swap cash for instruments on the Fed balance sheet.

Reverse repos peaked at $2.55T on Dec 30, 2022. Money gushed out from March 2023 to May 2024. The outflows have taken another leg down as the remaining balances have been drained. The balance is now at $5.9B (as of Dec. 23).

Figure 11: Fed Reverse Repurchase Agreements

Wrapping Up

The Fed is continuing to cut rates while inflation is moving up. With $38.5T in debt (and growing extremely fast), they have no choice. To keep the debt load sustainable, rates have to come down. Gold at $4,500 is screaming from the mountaintops that this is not a good idea. Gold does need some time to cool off, but there is nothing in the fundamentals that tells you the gold bull market is over. If anything, it is still in the early innings. Silver has lagged for years and is finally catching up. It also needs to take a much-needed breather, but dips should be considered buying opportunities.

Original Post

data.title: Janus Henderson Overseas ADR Managed Account Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
At a glance

Performance

The Portfolio returned 6.27% (gross) and the MSCI All Country World ex USA Index SM returned 7.03%.

Contributors/detractors

Stock selection in the communication services sector detracted from relative performance, while stock selection in the healthcare sector contributed.

Outlook

We will seek to use market volatility as we pursue opportunities where we believe the potential for free cash flow is undervalued.

Investment environment

  • Non-U.S. stocks rose strongly on market relief at a de-escalation of the trade war. Stocks in Japan and the emerging markets performed well, although European equities lagged after outperforming earlier in the year.
  • The market advance was relatively narrow. More cyclical sectors outperformed, while excitement around artificial intelligence (AI) aided performance by information technology stocks. The real estate sector underperformed.

Portfolio review

Beverage company Heineken (HEINY) was a detractor from relative performance. Shares declined even though the company reported better-than-expected earnings growth for the first half of the year. Investors were concerned about the recent sluggish pace of beer sales in developed markets, such as North American, which offset strength in emerging markets such as Vietnam. We have been reassured that this weakness has reflected short-term economic headwinds rather than longer-term structural shifts in consumption habits. We continue to believe in Heineken's brand strength and the opportunities around its global market expansion. In our view, the stock remains attractively valued relative to the company's expected earnings and cash-flow growth.

Deutsche Telekom (DTEGY), another relative detractor, is a leading telecommunications and information technology service provider in Europe. It also owns a majority stake in T-Mobile (TMUS) US (TMUS), one of the three leading U.S. wireless carriers. Shares of

Deutsche Telekom declined on concerns around management succession planning at T-Mobile. Srini Gopalan was promoted to chief operating officer, ahead of replacing longtime CEO Mike Sievert, a well-regarded leader who has grown the company's profitability and guided it through a merger with Sprint. We believe the market reaction to these developments was overstated, especially as the company raised its overall profit guidance in August. We also see opportunity stemming from T-Mobile's advantaged spectrum in the U.S., where we believe it is positioned to gain market share within the broader wireless market.

We have looked for opportunity with several European banks, which we believe may benefit from reduced regulation and increased industry consolidation. These included Banco Bilbao Vizcaya (BBVA), a top positive contributor during the quarter. A Spanish bank with a large business presence in Mexico, BBVA has delivered strong earnings growth, underpinned by resilient loan growth and high asset quality. It has looked to expand its business reach through its planned acquisition of rival Spanish bank Sabadell, which would make BBVA the third largest bank in Europe. BBVA's franchise in underbanked Mexico has also delivered resilient returns and strong fee growth.

Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) was another relative contributor. As one of the world's dominant chip manufacturers, TSMC is the leading supplier of graphic processing units to companies, such as NVIDIA (NVDA), that are at the forefront of the AI buildout. Excitement around the AI supply chain grew as leading hyperscalers and other technology companies continued to announce substantive investments in high performance computing. We believe TSMC is well positioned to capitalize on this spending due to its unparalleled scale, deep technological advantages, and its partnerships with major semiconductor companies and hyperscalers. The company has further expanded its market leadership as competitors have stumbled or shifted away from the foundry business, leaving TSMC as the unparalleled market leader in this space.

Manager outlook

We have been reassured by the continued growth in the global economy, and we have been pleased to see many of our companies make progress on their growth trajectories. While we acknowledge economic headwinds, we believe interest rate cuts in the U.S. and other markets could help spur increased global economic activity. We continue to find compelling investment opportunities across sectors and geographies, and we remain excited about innovations we have seen in areas such as information technology, healthcare, and energy production and storage. At the same time, we caution that valuations in some pockets of the market appear increasingly disconnected from underlying fundamentals.

In this environment, we believe active management is critical to managing risk. We remain committed to sifting through opportunities to identify companies positioned for superior free-cash-flow growth that we believe is undervalued by the market. Our focus remains on companies with exceptional management teams, high-quality balance sheets, strong competitive positioning, and high or improving returns on invested capital. We have taken steps to cushion the portfolio against potential market volatility by investing in companies with recurring revenues and earnings visibility that we believe can outperform across market environments. We view this disciplined, fundamentals-driven approach as key to delivering long-term capital appreciation for our investors.

data.title: IQDF Over IQDY: The Verdict On FlexShares' International Twins
data.description:
When looking for global opportunities, there are many different strategies out there to consider. I previously wrote positive articles about global dividend strategies, and today I will look at two of Northern Trust’s dividend ETFs with a quality focus: the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Index Fund (IQDF) and the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Dynamic Index Fund (IQDY). These two funds are leveraging a proprietary scoring model from the company to provide investors with a “smart beta” exposure, but I will give both of these funds hold ratings while noting that IQDF is a superior fund.

IQDY is a very small ETF that is completely uninvestable due to its catastrophic liquidity, while the larger IQDF, while not suffering from this “worst in class” liquidity, is simply not strong enough to justify its expense ratio, with a distribution yield barely above the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX) and an unattractive risk profile for an income ETF.

The Liquidity Chasm

As these two funds are so close, with similar goals and the same management company, a crucial metric is their operational efficiency. This is especially important as there is a huge gap in AUM between the two funds: the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Index Fund has AUM of $937 Mn, more than 10 times above the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Dynamic Index Fund’s $91 Mn. There is a direct correlation between the size of a fund and its liquidity, and we have here a good example of that. The average daily trading volume of IQDF is $3.2 Mn (0.34% of the fund), above IQDY’s $284 K (0.31% of the fund). The first thing to note here is that a “quality” ETF should be liquid, because such an ETF is designed to be a refuge against market volatility, and with such small volumes, I am afraid that the volatility in periods of market stress is not ensured.

On a daily basis, the median bid/ask spread of IQDF is 0.07%, which is already high, and considering the average daily trading volume, it could definitely widen in times of market stress. This is even worse for IQDY: the fund has a median bid/ask spread of 0.20%, making the fund completely untradable. The spread is almost half of the expense ratio, and the expense ratio is high. On a comparative basis, even if IQDY were a superior fund in terms of strategy (which I don’t think it is), this volatility issue alone would be enough to severely hurt the fund’s appeal. Given that there are a lot of ETFs aimed at global markets with far better liquidity and similar goals, this is enough to give a hold rating to IQDY.

The Cost of "Quality" and the Yield Disappointment

The common idea behind smart beta funds is that you are going to pay higher fees than for a beta strategy, but you are going to get superior returns by doing so. Both IQDF and IQDY are charging a 0.47% expense ratio. Over a well-known beta ETF like the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF and its expense ratio of 0.32%, this is a 0.15 percentage point premium that you pay to access the quality dividend strategy. For any of these two ETFs to be a buy, it would imply that the 0.15 percentage point premium is a worthy investment. As per the two funds’ names, one thing that you expect from the two ETFs is superior income streams.

I don’t think that they are very convincing at delivering income despite the dividend focus. To achieve “higher dividends,” IQDF is tracking the Northern Trust International Quality Dividend Index, and IQDY is tracking the Northern Trust International Quality Dividend Dynamic Index. The difference between the two indexes is that the first one filters for companies with “expected dividend payment and fundamental factors such as profitability, management efficiency, and cash flow,” while the second does the same but with a 50% higher targeted beta exposure.

While the two benchmarks are different, they both translate to a 3.29% dividend yield, which is only a 0.45 percentage point advantage against the market (ACWX’s dividend yield is 2.84%). When taking the expense ratio into account, this is a 0.30 percentage point difference. Not very convincing for a dividend-focused ETF with challenged liquidity metrics.

You could argue that the appeal of IQDF is not the dividend but the quality approach, but I don’t think that this is a convincing argument either. If we look at risk-adjusted returns measured by Sharpe ratios, we have 1.18 (3 years), 0.57 (5 years), and 0.43 (10 years) for IQDF and 1.09 (3 years), 0.57 (5 years), and 0.48 (10 years) for IQDY. As a reference, Sharpe ratios for ACWX are 0.82 (3 years), 0.40 (5 years), and 0.43 (10 years). Over the last three years, the quality tilt of IQDF has been strong, but the longer you go back in time, the less convincing it is. I think that the last few years on the markets were exceptional and that we are entering a market with new dynamics: trade tensions, geopolitical uncertainty, sticky inflation, localized recessionary risks, and multiple inflated tech markets amid AI bubble risks. What worked over the last three years but not over the very long term might not work in the next three years, and if risk-adjusted metrics over the long term are aligned with beta strategies, why pay the smart beta premium? My answer is don’t pay it.

The first thing to note about the two funds is that they are centered around financials. Over the last 3 years, this has been a very strong positioning at the core of the funds’ outperformance. But as we are entering 2026, this strong Financials exposure is introducing a sensitivity risk to interest rates like in the Eurozone and China. The actual trajectory is hard to predict, as central banks are pulling short-term interest rates down, but long-term rates are staying high due to inflationary fears. Overall, if this dynamic where long-term rates are staying high is sustained in 2026, the positioning of the two funds will remain relevant, but if interest rates are going down, net interest margins for the banking sector are going to be lowered, and this could hurt both funds’ performance. Now, I expect a strong financial exposure to dividend ETFs, but I would have preferred if the quality filter resulted in a more defensive tilt that would be more appropriate to navigate this evident risk.

While they have a similar exposure to their key sector, the fact that IQDY is a “dynamic” fund makes it more exposed to Industrials than IQDF. The dynamic scoring model of the fund is making it bet on the manufacturing and infrastructure rebound, aligned with global capex cycles. To do so, IQDY is investing less in defensive sectors such as staples and communication services: IQDY has an aggressive tilt to global markets, chasing cyclical growth, making it relevant in times of economic expansion.

Finally, both funds are invested in tech but with low allocations. This is, of course, consistent with the dividend mandate of the two funds, but considering their low yields, I would have expected a more aggressive positioning on growth themes similar to what you would find in a global “growth at a reasonable price” fund, and the tech industry is precisely where such a strategy is relevant. Tech companies are mostly not redistributing cash flows but reinvesting them into their activity to fuel growth, and this is not making them very attractive to income investors, but this is more true in the US than in the rest of the world, where you can find more mature companies with stronger capital redistribution plans overall. This is why the dividend yield of ACWX is so high, and I don’t really understand why IQDF and IQDY are not significantly higher considering their sectoral splits.

Looking at the geographic split, you can see that the two funds are actually very similar and especially aimed at three countries: Japan, the UK, and Taiwan.

Japan is the key geography in the two funds, and I think that it is very consistent with the recent corporate reforms of the country favoring shareholder returns through buybacks and dividends. For investors, this increases the appeal of the country as the country benefits from strong balance sheets with significant cash reserves and growing yields. This “safe” positioning might be a little offset by the weights in the UK, as the country has been lately marked by stagnating valuations, and the economy is still navigating the long-term consequences of Brexit. Of course, the yield appeal is here, but I am not so sure about the quality component, as the country is exposed to unique risks (which is also interesting for diversification purposes).

In addition, there is significant exposure to the Greater China region (China, Taiwan, and Hong Kong) with roughly 20% of the portfolio. This exposure is interesting and contrasts with many other global dividend ETFs centered around developed markets, and the quality filter appears crucial to investing in these geographies marked with geopolitical tensions. However, quantifiable corporate metrics cannot properly anticipate risks attached to sanctions, tariffs, or military tensions, and as a remuneration for this risk, I would have expected a higher dividend yield. I view this exposure as risks that we are not compensated for enough, and this comforts my hold rating.

The opposite thing can be said about the Western Europe exposure. Exposure to the Swiss equity market is a way to benefit from large multinationals with strong footprints in the global pharmaceutical and consumer-facing industries, especially true for IQDY, which is leveraging the “safe” reputation of the country to rationalize its cyclical sectoral exposure. For the two funds, the rest of the European exposure is split across many countries, with a long tail of small countries with smaller global footprints, more exposed to the local economic indicators. I don’t necessarily like this, as for me the appeal of Europe in dividend-focused funds is the fact that the region is home to mature multinationals trading at favorable valuation levels against US peers, and this is a good foundation for a global dividend fund.

Active Management in Disguise?

Smart beta strategies are often showing higher portfolio turnover than beta strategies, and I recognize that a high portfolio turnover for a passive ETF can be justified by the strategy. I find the 51% portfolio turnover of IQDF and the 61% of IQDY to be abnormally high.

The reason behind such high turnovers is the Quality Score model of Northern Trust, constantly re-evaluating companies for their management efficiency, profitability, and cash flow metrics, but such a high portfolio turnover implies that a company stays on average in the portfolio for two years, and it makes me wonder about the efficiency of the quality screening. Is Northern Trust’s model really scanning for quality companies with strong moats or just buying cyclical companies that temporarily screen well and making them rotate for other cyclical companies beating their score with short-term focuses?

Another factor that is making me ask this question is the weightings of both funds. IQDF holds 272 companies for a top 10 concentration of 16.52%, against 237 and 17.20% for IQDY. This is what I consider to be a good level of diversification, but this also dilutes both the impact of winners and losers, consistent with a low-conviction approach with a high portfolio turnover, but not really with a quality screening apparently so strict that it even caps the dividend yield. I would expect a good reason behind the relatively low dividend yield for an income dividend yield to be the strong conviction level attached to high-quality companies backed by a high conviction level, but this is not what I am seeing with either of the two funds.

Volatility without Reward

While Sharpe ratios appeared to be interesting earlier, it is actually because of the recent outperformance of the fund and not necessarily something consistent over time, as you can see from total returns:

As you can see, 3Y and 1Y returns are impressive, but going further actually shows returns aligned with the market. Now, as mentioned before, I don’t think that paying a premium for returns aligned with the market is really interesting, but it could be rationalized by risks and downside protection, something that you definitely have in mind when going for a “quality” ETF. I have bad news here: over the last 5 years, the maximum drawdown of IQDF was -28.24%, and the one of IQDY was -30.67%. As a reference, the figure for ACWX was -28.38%: the "quality" filter is not a defensive tool.

It does, however, result in lower valuation metrics: the P/E ratio of IQDF is 12.40 and its P/B ratio is 1.78, against 12.74 and 1.81 for IQDY. The fact that IQDY is slightly more expensive is consistent with the Dynamic style, and it remains lower than the 14.75 and 2.01 of ACWX. More than quality funds, I think that IQDF and IQDY are better considered as value funds.

Conclusion

The landscape of global ETFs is competitive, and I don’t think that either the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Index Fund or the FlexShares International Quality Dividend Dynamic Index Fund is competitive enough to be considered. Their quality focus is indeed interesting, but there are clear flaws. IQDY is excluded immediately from a buy rating because of its poor liquidity, while IQDF is doing better about liquidity but is not convincing enough about the rest of its metrics. I believe that to give a buy rating to a smart beta ETF, there should be a clear argument as to why this specific fund is superior to a beta fund, and I have not found such an argument with the fund’s inconsistent metrics.

data.title: BAE Systems: Underperformance Isn't What It Seems; Here's The Real Story
data.description:
European defense stocks remain in focus, driven by expanding European defense budgets and weapon system recapitalization, as well as stock price volatility driven by the prospects of fragile peace on the Eastern borders of Europe. For BAE Systems (BAESF, BAESY), I provided a $23.55 price target in my prior report. This stock price target has been reached, and we note that the stock price even advanced as much as 37% before the stock price returned to my more conservative price target.

The 10% upside was reached within two months, so while the current performance has not been impressive relative to the performance of the S&P 500, we do note that the price target was reached relatively fast, giving investors the opportunity to take some profit and rotate funds into other investment opportunities. In this report, I discuss how a ladder-type trimming of the position would have worked out, and I update my price target for the stock.

BAE Systems Seemingly Underperformed, But Investment Strategy Shows Outperformance

The image above creates the impression that the stock underperformed the broader market. However, I do believe the change against the benchmark does not tell the full story. If we would have sold the position strictly when the $23.55 price target was hit, we would have locked in the profit within 45 days. If after that we would have fully invested the cost basis and profits from the investment in the S&P 500, the return would have been 45%.

Alternatively, if we would like to be a bit more exposed to additional upside potential, we could have let the stock run up 10%, and for each 10% increase in the stock price against the cost basis, we would have sold a quarter of the position. By now, 75% of the position would have been sold, generating a 20% profit and performing in line with the market. If we were to re-invest that into the broader market, it would have resulted in 40.5% profit, including the 20% gain already locked in. Including the 25% position that was not sold since BAE Systems stock did not increase 40%, we are looking at a 32.8% gain. So, while it seems that BAE Systems stock did underperform, we do note that investing in BAE Systems stock and then either exiting the position fully or incrementally while investing the proceeds in the S&P 500 would have resulted in market-outperforming returns.

Another interesting performance figure is that by the time the 10% upside that I envisioned was realized, the S&P 500 had lost 8.7% of its value. So, it clearly demonstrates that a 7% return against a 19.9% benchmark return measured from the time of publication to the 24th of December 2025 does not tell a highly accurate story on the success of the pick.

Renewed European Security Landscape Drives Business Strength

For defense contractors, expanding defense budgets globally provide a major growth opportunity. For European companies, the added opportunity is the shift in the US-European relationship, which is leading to higher defense budgets potentially being used to source from European defense contractors. Furthermore, we are seeing increased European military readiness driving demand for services and logistics, and we are seeing more investment in new weapon system capabilities.

BAE Systems can benefit from a bump in defense budgets and a higher allocation for European contractors. The company can provide solutions for electronic warfare, submarines, armored vehicles, communication solutions, naval guns, artillery ammunition, radars, and aircraft carriers. It also has exposure to fighter jet programs, unmanned aerial vehicles, and loitering munitions. Furthermore, we note that the company is developing hypersonic missile capabilities as well as directed energy weapons, which could play a major role in hypersonic missile defense.

For the full year, the company expects 8% to 10% sales growth, or £30.6-£31.1 billion in sales, while EBIT is expected to grow 9% to 11% from the £3 billion produced last year, with free cash flow of £1.1 billion.

BAE Systems Buy Rating Is Maintained

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For BAE Systems, I am maintaining my buy rating. My previously established price target of $23.55 has been increased to $30, implying 27% upside. This is based on a valuation between the peer group median and BAE Systems' median EV/EBITDA for 2026, which is somewhat conservative given that we could also value the stock one year ahead of earnings. The increase in the price target is driven by slightly higher expected free cash flow in 2026 compared to our prior estimate and a higher peer group valuation, which drives up the undervaluation of BAE Systems compared to peers. We note that EBITDA is set to grow 6.6% annually through 2027 measured from 2024, while free cash flow performance will decline 2.6% annually. Measured from 2025, EBITDA is set to grow 8.3% annually, while free cash flow will grow 34.2% annually, which potentially will mark a more investment-heavy year for BAE Systems to invest in capacity and product portfolio.

For BAE Systems, we note that margins were exceptionally strong in 2024, and going forward, we will see a modest expansion in margins while sales will grow high single digits. Cash performance is expected to dip this year and pick up in the years ahead, while capital expenditures as a percentage of operating cash flow will remain elevated. The company is expected to continue growing shareholder returns, but a yield on cost at 2.3% by 2027 does not look impressive. Net debt leverage is expected to be stable this year and come down substantially in the years after.

Conclusion: BAE Systems Remains A Buy

BAE Systems is not a company that is expected to see substantial improvement in the margins, but it is expected to see high single-digit growth, which, combined with a wider valuation gap between peers, drives a higher price target. From a demand perspective, we do note that expansion in European defense budgets provides substantial growth opportunities that stretch beyond the forecast period. As potential risks, we note investments required to expand capacity and the fact that sentiment may sour on peace talks in Ukraine. However, we note that peace talks have shown little progress over recent months, and even if peace is achieved, it does not mean that European countries no longer need to invest in defense. As a result, I am maintaining my buy rating with a $30 price target.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: EXG: Attractively Valued Global Income Fund (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Overview

As the Santa rally continues, U.S. market indices continue to hover near their all-time highs. Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Global Diversified Equity Income Fund (EXG) provides investors with a way to reduce reliance on U.S. markets by allocating a sizeable portion of its assets to global securities outside of the U.S. When I previously covered EXG, I issued a hold rating due to the flawed dividend history. However, EXG released an updated annual report for 2025, so I wanted to take a closer look at the fund's overall value proposition for long-term investors. I believe that I may have been too strict on my expectations of the fund at the time of my first coverage and wanted to give it another honest look as we approach the new year.

When looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that EXG's share price has increased by about 13%. The fund has demonstrated its ability to participate in the positive market momentum over the last few quarters. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 23.7% over the same time frame. The awesome total return can be attributed to the starting dividend yield of about 8.3% and monthly payouts. This makes the fund an enticing choice for investors seeking a source of high-income generation from a global portfolio of equities.

While the fund prioritizes income generation, EXG relies on the growth of its equities and the included option writing strategy to support the distributions. This means that EXG will perform better during periods of positive market momentum while struggling to offer protection against severe declines. However, EXG trades at an attractive price-to-NAV valuation and may be a way for investors to reduce reliance on U.S. equities going forward. The fund has a mixed dividend history but a strong performance of out-earning its distributions. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that EXG implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, EXG has total net assets of $2.9B that are spread across 92 different positions. EXG takes a very specific approach to its portfolio and doesn't try to replicate the performance of the S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100. The fund has management fees of 0.99% of assets, which is fine considering the higher yield offered to investors. As the name implies, the fund also provides exposure to equities that have primary operations outside of the U.S. For reference, the fund's breakdown by region is as follows:

  • North America: 55.69%
  • Europe: 34.33%
  • Asia/Pacific: 7.56%
  • Latin America/Caribbean: 1.56%
  • Africa: 0.86%

Expanding on this diversity, EXG also provides instant exposure to a wide range of sectors. EXG is most heavily allocated to Information Technology sector at 24.84% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to Financials and Consumer Discretionary investments, which accounts for 17.94% and 13.06% of assets, respectively. The combination of these top three sectors makes up more than 55% of EXG's total assets, so investors should anticipate that these sectors will have the greatest impact on the fund's daily price change.

Additionally, the fund chooses to implement an option writing strategy against a portion of its assets in an effort to amplify the income received. Rather than including debt investments, the option writing strategy allows the fund to harness the volatility of its holdings and turn this into a positive trait. However, the fund generally keeps the option writing at a low frequency. For instance, the option writing characteristics are as follows:

  • EXG only writes options against 15% of its portfolio.
  • Written options have an average expiration of 14 days.
  • The fund estimates to only capture 0.3% of the price upside for written options.

While options can help generate income, they cap the upside growth momentum during market rallies. This can offset the fact that EXG is exposed to some of the highest quality growth companies in the world. For instance, Microsoft (MSFT) is the largest holding at 5.83% of assets. This is followed by an allocation to many other notable holdings such as Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), and ASML Holdings (ASML), just to name a few.

Valuation & Performance Risks

Since EXG is structured as a closed-end fund, the price can trade at a difference from the actual value of its underlying assets. As a result, EXG now trades at a discount to NAV of 6.55%. For reference, EXG has traded at an average discount to NAV of 6.1% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that the fund still trades at a reasonable price-to-NAV level in relation to its historical range. Although the discount may not be as attractive as it once was a few years ago, I think that the current levels are still a solid entry point for long-term investors.

However, I wanted to review the latest annual report for 2025 to get more clarity into the fund's internal performance. Net investment income amounted to $0.06 per share, while net realized gains landed at $1.31 per share. Looking back on prior years, EXG has historically reported weak net investment income levels. This is an indication that the fund doesn't really prioritize interest or dividend income as a way to fuel its earnings growth and NAV growth. Therefore, the fund is ultimately reliant on positive net realized gains to bridge this gap in earnings.

The challenge is that net realized gains can wildly vary based on market conditions. EXG relies on positive market momentum so that it can sell securities at a higher valuation than they were purchased for. Conversely, market declines will likely result in large net realized losses. This means that EXG doesn't really offer much protection against market declines. This vulnerability has resulted in stunted NAV growth during periods where market indices declined. Despite this, the fund has demonstrated heavy long-term growth over the last few years. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2021: $10.36 per share
  • 2022: $7.81 per share
  • 2023: $8.00 per share
  • 2024: $9.20 per share
  • 2025: $9.78 per share

Since the fund is most heavily allocated to technology companies, I anticipate that EXG will reward investors that are bullish about the outlook of the sector. For instance, Grand View Research estimates that the size of the AI market can expand at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033. While EXG can participate in this expansion since it has direct equity exposure to many of the companies leading the AI charge, long-term investors should limit their expectations. Due to the option writing strategy and the fact that the fund needs to sell off a portion of its gains to fuel distributions, the total returns are likely to underperform more traditional index-tracking ETFs.

For instance, the iShares Global 100 ETF (IOO) offers a straightforward way to get equity exposure on a global range. When comparing the performance over the last ten years, we can see that EXG's total return severely underperforms against IOO. Therefore, it's important that investors understand that a high dividend yield doesn't automatically translate to a high total return over time.

As previously noted, EXG is heavily reliant on net realized gains to support distributions. This means that EXG may severely underperform in a scenario where indices fall. If the fund cannot produce earnings that outpace distributions, EXG will find itself in a scenario where it is paying out more than it actually generates. This can lead to much larger losses than traditional ETFs that do not prioritize distributions. A good example of this can be demonstrated through 2022's market decline. As we can see below, EXG saw a much larger loss than IOO, since it is still paying out a large rate of distributions.

Variable But Solid Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.0657 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 8.3%. If we take this monthly payout and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $0.7884 per share. When referring to the last annual report, we can see that the fund may see some variability in its ability to produce earnings that exceed the distributions being paid. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2021: $3.03 per share
  • 2022: ($1.72) per share
  • 2023: $0.85 per share
  • 2024: $1.94 per share
  • 2025: $1.37 per share

So while the earnings can wildly vary based on market conditions, I believe that EXG is likely to continue supporting the distributions. Don't be fooled by the downward trend of payouts on the graph below. I believe that the variable payouts are a result of strong management that remains proactive in keeping the NAV secure over a long period of time. The fund has already demonstrated its ability to produce several years worth of earnings in a single period. A good example of this was demonstrated in 2024:

2024's earnings of $1.94 per share / $0.7884 annual payout = 2.46 years.

One of the most attractive aspects of the fund is the ability to pay out the majority of its distributions in a tax-efficient manner. For instance, the latest Section 19(a) notice indicates that 73.5% of the payouts YTD were fueled by net realized long-term capital gains. This means that most of the income received YTD was classified as qualified dividends, which have the most favorable tax treatment for investors. Therefore, EXG can be a great option for investors that want to generate income outside of a tax-advantaged account.

However, tax-efficient distributions don't necessarily equate to tax-free. For instance, 20.2% of distributions YTD were supported by net realized short-term gains, which are taxed at the highest rates for investors. Similarly, a smaller portion of payouts is also supported by net investment income, which also has the least favorable tax treatment. So investors that want to absolutely minimize their tax liability should utilize a position within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am upgrading my rating on EXG to a buy. After reviewing the latest annual report, the fund has demonstrated its ability to produce positive earnings that can support distributions. The fund still trades at a very reasonable price-to-NAV valuation that indicates some upside potential if AI continues to lead technology stocks higher. However, the caveat is that a position in EXG makes the most sense for investors that specifically want income generation. If you're an investor that simply wants the highest total return from a global portfolio of equities, a more traditional growth ETF may be the more efficient choice.

data.title: DGRO's 2025 Reconstitution Highlights Strong Dividend Growth
data.description:
Recap

I last covered iShares Core Dividend Growth ETF (DGRO) about a year ago for its 2024 annual reconstitution, and I'm back again covering this year's 2025 reconstitution. I added heavily to my DGRO position in 2025 and will continue doing so in 2026. Year to date, DGRO has performed well, delivering 15.6% returns while mostly keeping up with the S&P 500's 17.5% with less volatility. Both smoked my Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) position, but I don't mind as I own all three.

I was pretty nervous in the second half of the year about whether DGRO would be able to deliver yet another year of uninterrupted dividend growth, and it did, delivering an 18% increase compared to last year's Q4 dividend and a 4.73% annual increase overall for the year. Not the strongest of dividend growth years, but hey, it's more than my annual W-2 raises will give me. In any case, DGRO's methodology delivered yet again a solid year of performance with its methodology and annual reconstitution when they clean house.

Reconstitution Analysis

Let's dive into DGRO's 2025 annual reconstitution. For a refresher on the exact methodology, please refer to my reconstitution article from last year. For a quick recap, DGRO focuses on companies with consistent dividend increases and screens for sustainability. All holdings are US-based and pay a qualified dividend, with the top holdings being weighted by dividend dollars paid, capped at no more than 3% per holding. The holdings have been slowly reducing, which I like, from 435 in 2023, 415 in 2024, 410 in 2025, and now 406 going into 2026.

The new top 50 positions, which represent 66% of the ETF, are dominated by large-cap, blue-chip companies with a weighted average yield and DPS (dividend per share) growth of 2.24% and 7.80%, respectively. Above are the top 10 holdings, which make up 26% of the ETF. The top 30 holdings by dividend growth represent 6.7% weight with an average yield of 1.70%, lower than the overall ETF yield, with a DPS growth of 23%, reflecting dividend growth over yield. Notable dividend growers were The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS) and JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), with a blistering DPS growth of 33.33% and 20%, respectively.

44 companies were added, representing about 7% of the ETF's weight, mostly driven by the five-year continuous dividend growth screen. The average yield is in line with the majority weight at 2.59% and slightly higher dividend growth of 8.51%. Philip Morris International Inc. (PM) and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) stood out as pretty notable adds. 43 companies were removed, representing about 5.4% of the ETF's weight. The removals were mostly driven by "extreme dividend yield." An interesting insight is the average yield of these removed holdings was 4.34%, but their dividend growth was only 5.25%. I won't be missing U.S. Bancorp (USB), Target Corporation (TGT), Comcast Corporation (CMCSA), or General Mills, Inc. (GIS). In my opinion, these are companies that are socially and operationally on a downward trend. These adds and removals are a small weight overall, keeping turnover low and reflecting a strong prior portfolio yet again.

Overall, I believe this reconstitution emphasizes the strong dividend growth profile of DGRO. Technology and Health Care dominate the top holdings for overall growth, with Utilities and Energy keeping the yield above the S&P 500. The yield is still pretty conservative, but I believe the 2026 dividend growth is set up well to keep the uninterrupted dividend growth streak alive and possibly get into double-digit growth.

Outlook

DGRO and SCHD are a substantial part of my taxable brokerage portfolio and the real core of my income strategy. I auto-buy $50 of each ETF every single day and feel very comfortable scaling these holdings into multi-million-dollar positions one day. In fact, I don't know how else I will retire comfortably if I don't do this. At around $70/share, I think there's plenty of value even at today's entry price than there was a year ago due to the price appreciation, dividend growth, and overall growth set up for next year. One thing I do find myself getting frustrated with on DGRO is the low yield. I want a 4%+ yield now, but we have to be patient and let the yield on cost do its thing with each year's annual raise. That's why I buy NEOS funds in my business brokerage account and am trying to buy one Bitcoin via NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI) to help offset this frustration and increase my current income.

While I track the total returns of my holdings against the market and other competing funds, I'm very much focused on scaling my dividends in my portfolio. My goal is highly tax-efficient passive income to offset all my fixed expenses: subscriptions, cellphone, water, internet, gas, energy, car & home insurance, CapEx, property taxes, and grocery bills, and one day, all my more variable and fun living expenses to live a comfortable and secure life.

data.title: GoGold: A Good Value Even With More Conservative Metal Prices
data.description:
Overview

GoGold Resources (GLGDF)(GGD:CA) is a Canadian-listed silver mining company with three assets in Mexico. Parral is its smallest asset, which is effectively a cleanup of old tailings that is producing around 2 million silver equivalent ("AgEq") ounces per year. The company also has the two development projects, Los Ricos South and Los Ricos North, which account for most of the company's value.

There was some optimization work a couple of years ago on Parral, which reduced production for a period, but the asset has, over the last year, been producing around 500Koz AgEq per quarter, and the silver equivalent cash cost has been around $18-19/oz over the last few years. Based on the current spot price of silver or just the $41.55/oz realized silver price in the most recent quarter, the margin is very healthy for Parral. This asset is effectively funding the general & administrative expenses and development costs of the other assets.

I have covered GoGold frequently going back to 2018, when it was first added to my portfolio, and my prior articles can be found here. I am still long the stock. The stock price is up 181% this year, which is roughly in line with the precious metals mining ETFs.

This is no doubt a healthy return for GoGold, but I still think the stock price has underperformed the expectations some in 2025. That is probably true for many silver stocks, where I would expect their returns to be more explosive over the last 6 months, when the price of silver has roughly doubled, and that does especially apply to GoGold.

I think the main reason GoGold has "underperformed" is because the pending permit on Los Ricos South has yet to be granted, and the other reason is the recent C$144M bought deal that closed in November.

There is no clear timeline of the permit approval, but the company has communicated that it is close. I think GoGold has a good chance of getting it sorted in the next 6 months following the recent open pit permit approval for Silver Tiger Metals (SLVTF), after a long period of few new mining permits being granted. The Los Ricos projects have good local support, which is key for most mining projects.

GoGold has done a good job of timing its bought deals over the years, which are highlighted in the chart below. I understand the rationale for the recent bought deal, as it will allow GoGold to move forward with both Los Ricos South and Los Ricos North quickly, without extending the balance sheet too much. With that said, I don't think a half-warrant would have been needed if management had been more patient with this capital raise. GoGold had no debt and over $100M prior to the bought deal and now has around $240M in cash on its books.

Valuation

GoGold had a feasibility study released in January of this year on Los Ricos South, making those estimates reliable. For Parral, we can quite easily discount the cash flow of its operation as well. However, the latest technical report on Los Ricos North was a preliminary economic assessment released in the first half of 2023, which makes those estimates more unreliable.

Historically, I have also assumed permitting Los Ricos North as an open pit project might be difficult, which is probably no longer the case following the recent permit approvals in Mexico. With that said, I have still been somewhat conservative with my estimate for Los Ricos North.

In the chart below, I have used two metal price scenarios and a 5% discount rate for the valuation. One could certainly make a case for a higher discount rate, but similarly for more aggressive metal prices, especially for silver. The first price scenario uses a silver price of $40/oz and a gold price of $3,500/oz; the second scenario uses a silver price of $50/oz and a gold price of $4,000/oz.

Here we can see that the total value of GoGold's various assets is $1.7B and $2.3B for the two metal price scenarios. The fully diluted enterprise value is $714M for GoGold, which means the stock is trading at 31% or 42% of total asset values depending on the price scenario we use.

Conclusion

GoGold is a relatively high-quality silver development company. The balance sheet and liquidity position are extremely good; the projected operating costs of the two Los Ricos projects are in the lowest quartile of the cost curve for silver projects. This is also a management team that has built mines before, offsetting the execution risk some.

Given how few high-quality silver projects there are with near-term production capabilities, I think this is a compelling stock price for GoGold. I rate the stock a buy, and I have been adding more money to my position in GoGold recently.

The slightly rushed recent bought deal is a minor issue in my view, which will be forgotten in a few months. The main risk is without a doubt the permitting risk in Mexico, where this discount was likely warranted a year ago but probably not anymore when mining permits have started to be issued in 2025 again. With that said, the binary risk of a mining permit on Los Ricos South is something all investors should be aware of.

data.title: S&P Global: Intelligence Player Intelligent Here?
data.description:
In the spring I concluded that S&P Global (SPGI) had become an impressive information powerhouse. Following the IHS Markit merger, the company has become a $14 billion information powerhouse, yet demanding valuations made me a bit cautious.

Ever since, shares have risen some 15%, and while they mark decent returns given such a period, they have been lagging the market returns by a substantial degree.

Right now shares still command premium multiples amidst solid returns seen, driven by a resilient operating performance. Given this backdrop and continued question of what AI has for an impact on the business, I am still erring cautiously here.

An Information Powerhouse

S&P Global looks to power global markets, with its information provision and services accelerating progress made in such markets. The company generates about $14 billion in sales, of which about a third is generated from market intelligence solutions and about a third from rating activities (which are a bit more cyclical), as well as commodity insight, mobility, and indices products.

The business was formerly known as McGraw-Hill, which tied its operations up with IHS Market in a $44 billion deal in 2020 (although closing only took place in 2022). The business is incredibly profitable, posting operating margins in the forties.

Back To Spring

Shares of S&P Global traded at $463 in April, already commanding premium valuations. This followed the business posting a 14% increase in 2024 sales to $14.2 billion, amidst strong operating momentum, notably in the rating business. Adjusted earnings were up a quarter to $15.70 per share; that number looked quite clean.

With net debt reported just shy of $10 billion, while EBITDA came in a long way to $7 billion, leverage was no issue in the mid-1s. The company guided for 2025 sales to advance by 5-7%, with adjusted earnings seen up modestly to $17.00-$17.25 per share.

The 309 million shares granted the company a $143 billion equity valuation in April and a $153 billion enterprise valuation. This is equal to about 10 times sales and 27 times adjusted earnings going forward. The company furthermore announced the sale of its 50/50 joint venture OSTTRA, this unit finding a new home at KKR. Given a $3.1 billion deal tag, S&P Global was set to fetch $1.5 billion in proceeds, making this a bolt-on divestment amidst continued streamlining efforts post the mega merger.

Simply, the company has a sticky business model, one with a solid track record, all commanding premium valuations. Having proven naysayers wrong for a long period of time, I feared the setup of high expectations and margins, together with the potential overhang of AI emerging.

Modest Gains

Since the spring, shares of S&P Global have largely traded in a $450-$550 price range, now trading at $525 per share.

Along with the first quarter results in April, S&P Global announced its intention to spin off S&P Global Mobility, targeted to become a standalone public company. This is a smaller part of the business, generating about $1.6 billion in sales in 2024, those being growing sales. The anticipated spin-off into a potentially publicly separate business was considered to be completed in 12-18 months; this is likely to be seen taking place in the second half of 2026.

With quarterly results coming in line with expectations, the company announced its next M&A move in October. In the middle of the month, S&P announced a $1.8 billion acquisition of With Intelligence, a provider of private markets data, insights, and analytics. Its solutions provide data to investors, managers, and funds and cover over 300,000 deals. With a $130 million revenue contribution, the purchase is expensive at nearly 14 times sales.

By the end of the month, the company reported third-quarter results, with a net debt load reported at $9.7 billion, despite the company fetching proceeds from selling-off OSSTRA. By now the company sees sales up 7-8%, with adjusted earnings between $17.60 and $17.85 per share, aided by resilient performance and a weaker dollar.

Commanding a $170 billion enterprise valuation, S&P Global traded around 12 times sales and about 30 times adjusted earnings per share, all still demanding valuations.

These valuations, however, are driven by continued growth in combination with capital allocation strategies. The company claims that AI is a driver for the business. I still see this as a potential threat as well, making me still quite cautious, at least for now, even as the company powers on.

data.title: Comex: Physical Silver Demand Keeps The Market In Backwardation
data.description:
The CME Comex is the Exchange where futures are traded for gold, silver, and other commodities. The CME also allows futures buyers to turn their contracts into physical metal through delivery. You can find more detail on the CME here (e.g., vault types, major/minor months, delivery explanation, historical data, etc.).

The data below looks at contract delivery where the ownership of physical metal changes hands within CME vaults. It also shows data that details the movement of metal in and out of CME vaults. It is very possible that if there is a run on the dollar, and a flight into gold, this is the data that will show early warning signs.

Gold

The chart below shows the spread between the spot price of gold and the futures contract pricing. As you can see, it blew out earlier this year (increasing contango). This created the massive arbitrage that resulted in gold traveling from London to New York. When the spread between futures and spot gets distorted, it suggests there is dislocation in the market. The spread normalized some between August and early December, but has started to blow out again. It’s possible this could lead to increased delivery volume again, but we have not seen that yet this month.

Figure 1: Spot vs. Futures

As shown below, while gold delivery volume is elevated on a historical basis, it is below the three massive delivery months earlier this year (Feb, Apr, and Oct). If spreads continue to blow out, then Jan and Feb could show large delivery volume again.

Figure 2: Recent like-month delivery volume

Below is a chart that isolates the month of December going back 30 years. We can see that on a notional level, this December is absolutely smashing historical records. While this is heavily impacted by the surging price (notional is price x volume), it still shows that the appetite for gold in dollars is increasing dramatically.

Figure 3: Notional Deliveries

Net new contracts (contracts that open and settle for immediate delivery) was a modest driver. Not as large as other months, but also a late surge that is not usually seen. That surge came as the spread started to widen (Figure 1). It’s likely that these are related.

Figure 4: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Perhaps the most important data point is the actual metal leaving Comex vaults. When metal is “delivered,” it does not mean it’s leaving the Comex. It means that Registered ownership is moving from one party to another. When the metal leaves the vault, that is where things get interesting. As shown, this year saw a major influx as the arbitrage trade took hold, but that metal was flowing back out. In the latest month, a lot of Eligible was converted to Registered to satisfy delivery demand. The Comex is not (yet?) bringing in any new metal.

Figure 5: Inventory Data

Looking ahead to the December delivery period, we see a contract that is hovering near the middle range of all minor months.

Figure 6: Open Interest Countdown

With the massive surge in inventory, the open interest relative to physical stocks is actually lower. This shows how much metal has been added to Comex vaults this year.

Figure 7: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Bottom line, December saw elevated deliveries on a historical level, but well below the 2025 highs. The Comex vaults saw little metal arrive or leave this month; it was mainly a conversion of Eligible into Registered.

Silver

While the gold market is showing a futures price that is getting higher above the spot rate (which caused metal to move from London to New York), the silver market is experiencing the opposite problem, backwardation. The spot price is above the futures price. This is actually a bigger warning sign because it highlights the pressure being put on the physical spot market.

Market Signal: Backwardation often signals a strong immediate demand or a supply shortage in the current spot market. The market is willing to pay a premium for the asset now, as the shortage is typically expected to resolve over the long term.

Figure 8: Spot vs. Futures

While gold delivery volume has cooled some, the silver delivery volume remains elevated.

Figure 9: Recent like-month delivery volume

Switching to notional values, and focusing on the month of December, produces the chart below. Again, some of this is price appreciation, but it is also an increased appetite for deliveries. In 30 years of delivery data, the notional amount is almost 2.5x higher than any previous December on record. The price appreciation is not denting the demand for physical.

Figure 10: Notional Deliveries

Unlike gold, net new contracts were a major driver this month, reaching the second highest behind only September.

Figure 11: Cumulative Net New Contracts

Silver-eligible inventories are seeing some modest inflows this month, but this is not new metal... it is metal coming from Registered as we are about to see.

Figure 12: Inventory Data

Registered metal (metal available for delivery) is seeing major outflows. Since September, there has been a massive drawdown in Registered supplies. This is further evidence of strain in the physical market. Investors are taking delivery and moving it to Eligible this month. In prior months it was coming straight out of the vault. This will be the chart to watch as we head into 2026.

Figure 13: Inventory Data

As we approach January delivery, the silver contract is now at the highest open interest when compared to all prior minor months. The deviation from the trend is stark.

Figure 14: Open Interest Countdown

On a relative basis, open interest is still in record territory despite the elevated inventory levels.

Figure 15: Open Interest Countdown Percent

Conclusion

The gold market has certainly calmed down when compared to the activity earlier this year. That said, with spreads widening, we may be in store for some more fireworks. Earlier this year, metal flowed into Comex vaults to satisfy demand that was coming from traders looking to arbitrage.

Silver is showing opposite behavior with spot prices above futures. This is causing metal to flow out of the Comex vaults. Heading into January, demand is staying very strong. While silver prices have gotten extended to the upside, any pullback should be well supported by physical demand.

data.title: Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the third quarter of 2025.

Fund performance

  • Institutional Class shares of Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund returned 3.72% for the three months ending September 30, 2025. For monthly performance information, please check online at columbiathreadneedleus.com .
  • The fund's benchmark, the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index (BMB), returned 3.00% for the same period.
  • The fund outperformed its benchmark for the quarter, as the fund's longer-duration profile was additive to returns. (Duration is a measure of a bond's sensitivity to changes in interest rates)

Market overview

The third quarter began with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act clearing both chambers of Congress and being signed into law on July 4 by President Trump. Throughout the quarter, tariff policy headlines continued, yet market reactions to trade policy announcements decreased relative to the previous quarter. While inflation and growth uncertainty remained, the U.S. Federal Reserve shifted its focus to the labor market, which showed signs of weakness throughout the period. The Fed viewed the downside risks to employment as meriting some degree of easing, despite inflation that remained somewhat elevated. After leaving its benchmark overnight lending rate unchanged at its August meeting, the Fed lowered the federal funds target rate in mid-September by a quarter point, to the 4.00%–4.25% range. Returns were meaningfully positive across all sectors of the U.S. bond market for the quarter. Treasury yields ended the quarter slightly lower across the yield curve, as reflected in the decline of the 10-year Treasury yield from 4.24% to 4.16% over the quarter.

Municipals outperformed Treasuries during the quarter. The Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index returned 3.00%, while Treasuries were up 1.51% over the same period. Lower-quality municipals lagged higher-quality municipals as the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index (BHY) returned 1.63% during the quarter, bringing its year-to-date return to 1.29%. A dominant theme from the first half of 2025 was the reshaping and ultimate steepening of the municipal yield curve. During the third quarter, the 1–30-year AAA slope flattened by 11 basis points (bps). (A basis point is 1/100 of one percent.) Longer-maturity municipal bonds materially outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts.

On the technical front, supply continued to expand at a record-setting pace. New issuance totaled $150 billion during the quarter, 6% higher year over year. The typically strong summer-month technicals were late to materialize, although the market's seasonality has not performed according to the usual pattern this year. After a modestly negative second quarter in terms of flows, fund flows increased throughout the third quarter. Open-ended funds experienced $8.3 billion in inflows, while exchange traded funds (ETFs) saw $10.1 billion in inflows for the quarter. The short and intermediate portions of the yield curve benefited most from the positive flows.

Top sector weights (%) as of September 30, 2025

Quarterly portfolio recap

Columbia Strategic Municipal Income Fund outperformed its benchmark during the quarter. As longer-maturity bonds outperformed their shorter-maturity counterparts, the fund's longer-duration profile was additive to returns. In terms of quality, security selection in AAA and AA rated bonds contributed to returns, while the fund's overweight to BB bonds detracted. Within housing and zero-coupon bonds, security selection was additive to returns, while selection in tobacco negatively impacted returns. Also detracting from returns were overweights to continuing care retirement community (CCRC) and housing bonds.

Portfolio activity

Throughout the quarter, the fund capitalized on opportunities in both the new-issue and secondary markets. Purchases were largely focused on improving the quality, liquidity and distribution yield of the fund. The strategy took advantage of attractive opportunities when volatility presented, while also decreasing duration during strong rally environments. While municipal spreads are not overly attractive from a valuation standpoint, yields remain desirable on both a historical and tax-equivalent basis. Due to ample supply in the market and higher income opportunities being offered, the fund was able to capitalize on attractive credits in several sectors. The relatively steep slope of the municipal yield curve, especially the longer end, continues to provide higher income in a stable-rate environment, as well as total return potential.

Outlook

We remain optimistic on our outlook for the municipal market, while also noting the fall season typically brings a more-challenging technical environment, with the lack of reinvestment from coupon and interest payments. We also expect issuance will continue at a record pace, and we remain optimistic about locking in attractive opportunities at elevated yields. Municipal/Treasury yield ratios are mixed, yet are attractive for potential crossover buyers, especially in longer maturities. While credit spreads remain compressed, we continue to prefer higher-quality over lower-quality credits, particularly as we see some areas of economic weakness and cracks beginning to emerge in the labor market. The relative steepness of the yield curve compensates investors for increasing maturity, and we maintain our conviction to a longer-duration stance vs. the benchmark. Fundamentals remain on a solid footing. However, default rates are picking up slightly (albeit from very low levels), so credit research and active management will remain imperative to success.
